summary: My name is Saymoukda Vongsay, and I am a Lao American poet and playwright. Over the past eight years, much of my work has focused on exploring my parents' journey and the stories they couldn't fully express about the war. I wrote a poem called "When everything was everything" that reflects my childhood experiences growing up as a refugee in Saint Paul and Minneapolis. This poem won the Alfred C. Carey Prize in Spoken Word Poetry in 2010, which I am very proud of.

Growing up as a refugee, parts of my childhood took place in Saint Paul, Minnesota. My poem "When everything was everything" includes vivid memories from this city, such as crossing the bridge over Highway 94 and walking through neighborhoods like St. Albans. Saint Paul was one of the places where my family struggled but also found moments of joy and community. It shaped much of my early experience and the stories I tell about my upbringing.

Minneapolis is another city where I spent parts of my childhood as a refugee. Along with Saint Paul, it forms the backdrop of many memories I captured in my poem "When everything was everything." Growing up in Minneapolis meant navigating poor resource neighborhoods and working hard alongside my family. This city is deeply connected to my identity and the stories I share about my family's immigrant experience.

Hudson, Wisconsin, is a place my family often visited during my childhood. In my poem, I describe riding in my father's tan Isuzu truck to Hudson, where my mom would buy lottery tickets and snacks like Funyuns and giant Slurpees for us. These trips were small but meaningful moments that brought joy to our otherwise challenging life as refugees. Hudson represents a part of my family's journey and the simple pleasures we shared.

My father's tan Isuzu truck was an important part of my childhood memories. We often drove in it to places like Hudson, Wisconsin, where my mom bought snacks and lottery tickets. The truck was like our family's gold chariot, as my brother Bhet would say, and it symbolized our journey and the small adventures we had together. It appears vividly in my poem "When everything was everything."

In 2010, I was honored to receive the Alfred C. Carey Prize in Spoken Word Poetry from New York for my poem "When everything was everything." This recognition meant a lot to me because the poem was a very honest reflection of my family's refugee experience and my childhood. Winning this prize helped validate the stories I wanted to tell about growing up in challenging circumstances but also finding beauty and resilience.

I remember reading from the book The Cay in my first-grade classroom. During ESL sessions, my classmates would giggle and snicker when I read aloud, but one day I read two chapters without stopping, which surprised everyone and stopped the teasing. This moment was important for me because it marked a turning point in how I was treated in school. The Cay became part of my early education experience and helped me gain confidence.

In my brother Bhet's first-grade classroom, there was a picture of Jesus Christ hanging on the wall. Passing by it was part of the daily routine as I followed Bhet to his class. This image was a small but notable detail in the school environment I experienced growing up. It represents the cultural and religious symbols present in the public schools I attended.
